text.title: event-driven-architecture Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for event-driven-architecture as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Publisher names an event imperatively (ProcessPayment, SendEmail) and expects only one specific consumer to act on it.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Commands have exactly one intended handler. Using an event channel for a command couples the producer to the consumer's presence, defeating the decoupling purpose of EDA.

failure-mode.description: Event payload contains full embedded objects rather than IDs. Example: OrderPlaced event contains the entire customer profile, full product catalog entries, and complete shipping address history.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Large events bloat the broker, slow consumers, make schema evolution harder, and couple producers to consumer data needs. Consumers that need full objects should fetch them separately via ID.

failure-mode.description: Events do not carry a correlation_id or trace context. When a flow fails across 5 services and 3 brokers, there is no way to reconstruct the event chain.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Distributed tracing without correlation IDs requires log archaeology. A single missing correlation_id makes debugging a distributed transaction blind and time-consuming.

failure-mode.description: Events named UserUpdated, OrderModified, ProductChanged — CRUD verbs applied to aggregate state.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: CRUD events lose business intent. OrderModified could mean a shipping address change, an item added, or a cancellation — consumers cannot determine what to do without parsing the full payload.
